methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 2 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Oklo is a public-stage nuclear energy company currently valued at $10B+, having raised $500M+ to date. The nuclear energy sector comprises a fragmented competitor landscape of 3 public peers and 8 private companies, including advanced reactor developers such as TerraPower, Kairos Power, X-Energy, Nano Nuclear Energy, and Zap Energy.

The peer-median valuation method is not applicable to this analysis. The nuclear energy sector contains only 7 same-sector peers total, and filtering by stage, adjacency, and valuation tier yields fewer than the 3 minimum datapoints required for a reliable median benchmark. Specifically, only 1 same-stage comparable, 0 adjacent-stage comparables, and 2 similar-tier comparables were identified—an insufficient dataset to support peer-median triangulation. Relying on all-sector medians would introduce material methodological error given the sector's heterogeneity and the specialized capital requirements of advanced nuclear development.

Without triangulation via peer-median analysis, alternative valuation methods—such as discounted cash flow, revenue multiples, or precedent transactions—would be necessary to establish a defensible range. The current $10B+ valuation reflects Oklo's public-market pricing and cumulative capital deployment, but derivation of an independent value range requires either: expansion of the comparable set through stage or sector relaxation (with explicit caveats), or application of fundamental-analysis methods anchored to the company's technology roadmap and commercialization timeline. Key uncertainties include regulatory approval timelines and unit economics at scale.
